question_text: Give reasons for the following observations: p-Chloronitrobenzene reacts with (aq) NaOH at 443 K to give p-nitrophenol whereas chlorobenzene reacts with the same reagent at 623 K and 300 atm. Main product obtained when chloroethane reacts with KCN is propane nitrile while with Ag CN it is ethyl isocyanide.
explanation: Due to electron withdrawing nature of {tex}-{NO}_2{/tex} group. However, AgCN is mainly covalent in nature and nitrogen is free to donate electron pair forming isocyanide as the main product.

explanation: Because of the stabilisation of intermediate carbocation through resonance. Through resonance, chlorine tends to stabilize the carbocation and the effect is more pronounced at ortho- and para- positions. solution: Because of the stabilisation of intermediate carbocation through resonance. Through resonance, chlorine tends to stabilize the carbocation and the effect is more pronounced at ortho- and para- positions. Because mixture contains two enantiomers in equal proportionsresulting in Zero Optical Rotation. / Due to the external compensation of equal percentage of (+) and (-) forms resulting in Zero Optical Rotation. Because of the resonance stabilization of allyl carbocation.

1. Because of the stabilisation of intermediate carbocation through resonance. Through resonance, chlorine tends to stabilize the carbocation and the effect is more pronounced at ortho- and para- positions.
  2. Because mixture contains two enantiomers in equal proportionsresulting in Zero Optical Rotation. / Due to the external compensation of equal percentage of (+) and (-) forms resulting in Zero Optical Rotation.
  3. Because of the resonance stabilization of allyl carbocation.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Nucleophilic Substitution

Nucleophilic Substitution reaction of haloalkane can be conducted according to both SN1 and SN2 mechanisms. SN1 is a two step reaction while SN2 is a single step reaction. For any haloalkane which mechanism is followed depends on factors such as structure of haloalkane, properties of leaving group, nucleophilic reagent and solvent.
Influences of solvent polarity : In SN1 reaction, the polarity of the system increases from the reactant to the transition state, because a polar solvent has a greater effect on the transition state than the reactant, thereby reducing activation energy and accelerating the reaction. In SN2 reaction, the polarity of the system generally does not change from the reactant to the transition state and only charge dispersion occurs. At this time, polar solvent has a great stabilizing effect on Nu than the transition state, thereby increasing activation energy and slow down the reaction rate. For example, the decomposition rate (SN1) of tertiary chlorobutane at 25oC in water (dielectric constant 79) is 300000 times faster than in ethanol (dielectric constant 24). The reaction rate (SN2) of 2-Bromopropane and NaOH in ethanol containing 40% water is twice slower than in absolute ethanol. Hence the level of solvent polarity has influence on both SN1 and SN2 reaction, but with different results. Generally speaking weak polar solvent is favourable for SN2 reaction, while strong polar solvent is favourable for SN1. Generally speaking the substitution reaction of tertiary haloalkane is based on SN1 mechanism in solvents with a strong polarity (for example ethanol containing water).

S N 1 is a two step reaction while S N 2 is a single step reaction. For any haloalkane which mechanism is followed depends on factors such as structure of haloalkane, properties of leaving group, nucleophilic reagent and solvent. Influences of solvent polarity : In S N 1 reaction, the polarity of the system increases from the reactant to the transition state, because a polar solvent has a greater effect on the transition state than the reactant, thereby reducing activation energy and accelerating the reaction. In S N 2 reaction, the polarity of the system generally does not change from the reactant to the transition state and only charge dispersion occurs. At this time, polar solvent has a great stabilizing effect on Nu than the transition state, thereby increasing activation energy and slow down the reaction rate. For example, the decomposition rate (S N 1) of tertiary chlorobutane at 25 o C in water (dielectric constant 79) is 300000 times faster than in ethanol (dielectric constant 24). The reaction rate (S N 2) of 2-Bromopropane and NaOH in ethanol containing 40% water is twice slower than in absolute ethanol. Hence the level of solvent polarity has influence on both S N 1 and S N 2 reaction, but with different results. Generally speaking weak polar solvent is favourable for S N 2 reaction, while strong polar solvent is favourable for S N 1. Generally speaking the substitution reaction of tertiary haloalkane is based on S N 1 mechanism in solvents with a strong polarity (for example ethanol containing water). question_html:
The substitution reaction of alkyl halide mainly occurs by SN1 or SN2 mechanism. Whatever mechanism alkyl halides follow for the substitution reaction to occur, the polarity of the carbon halogen bond is responsible for these substitution reactions. The rate of SN1 reactions are governed by the stability of carbocation whereas for SN2 reactions steric factor is the deciding factor. If the starting material is a chiral compound, we may end up with an inverted product or racemic mixture depending upon the type of mechanism followed by alkyl halide. Cleavage of ethers with HI is also governed by steric factor and stability of carbocation, which indicates that in organic chemistry, these two major factors help us in deciding the kind of product formed.

question_html: Write the structure of an isomer of compound C4H9Br which is most reactive towards SN1 reaction.
question_text: Write the structure of an isomer of compound C 4 H 9 Br which is most reactive towards S N 1 reaction.
explanation: Tertiary (3°) alkyl halides are most reactive towards S N 1 reactions followed by secondary (2°) and primary (1 o ) alkyl halides. C 4 H 9 Br has four isomers: CH 3 -CH 2 -CH 2 -CH 2 Br 1-bromobutane (1 o alkyl halide) {tex}C{H_3}C{H_2} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{Br} } H - C{H_3}{/tex} 2-bromobutane (2 o alkyl halide) {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } H - C{H_2}Br{/tex} 1-bromo-2-methyl propane (1 o alkyl halide) {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - Br{/tex} 2-bromo-2-methyl propane (3 o alkyl halide) As 3 o alkyl halides form a morestable 3 o carbocation intermediate than secondary and primary alkyl halides which form 2 o and 1 o carbocation, respectively. Hence, the isomer, {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - Br{/tex} (2-bromo-2-methyl propane) of compound C 4 H 9 Br is most reactive towards S N 1 reactions.
solution: Tertiary (3°) alkyl halides are most reactive towards S N 1 reactions followed by secondary (2°) and primary (1 o ) alkyl halides. C 4 H 9 Br has four isomers: CH 3 -CH 2 -CH 2 -CH 2 Br 1-bromobutane (1 o alkyl halide) {tex}C{H_3}C{H_2} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{Br} } H - C{H_3}{/tex} 2-bromobutane (2 o alkyl halide) {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } H - C{H_2}Br{/tex} 1-bromo-2-methyl propane (1 o alkyl halide) {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - Br{/tex} 2-bromo-2-methyl propane (3 o alkyl halide) As 3 o alkyl halides form a morestable 3 o carbocation intermediate than secondary and primary alkyl halides which form 2 o and 1 o carbocation, respectively. Hence, the isomer, {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - Br{/tex} (2-bromo-2-methyl propane) of compound C 4 H 9 Br is most reactive towards S N 1 reactions.
solution_html:
Tertiary (3°) alkyl halides are most reactive towards SN1 reactions followed by secondary (2°) and primary (1o) alkyl halides.
C4H9Br has four isomers:

  1. CH3-CH2-CH2-CH2Br
    1-bromobutane
    (1oalkyl halide)
  2. {tex}C{H_3}C{H_2} - \mathop C\limits_{\mathop |\limits_{Br} } H - C{H_3}{/tex}
    2-bromobutane
    (2oalkyl halide)
  3. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop C\limits^{\mathop |\limits^{C{H_3}} } H - C{H_2}Br{/tex}
    1-bromo-2-methyl propane
    (1oalkyl halide)
  4. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - Br{/tex}
    2-bromo-2-methyl propane (3o alkyl halide)

As 3o alkyl halides form a morestable 3o carbocation intermediate than secondary and primary alkyl halides which form 2o and 1o carbocation, respectively. Hence, the isomer,
{tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - Br{/tex} (2-bromo-2-methyl propane) of compound C4H9Br is most reactive towards SN1 reactions.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give reasons:

  1. C-Cl bond length in chlorobenzene is shorter than C-Cl bond length in CH3-Cl.
  2. The dipole moment of chlorobenzene is lower than that of cyclohexyl chloride.
  3. SN1 reactions are accompanied by racemization in optically active alkyl halides.
question_text: Give reasons: C-Cl bond length in chlorobenzene is shorter than C-Cl bond length in CH 3 -Cl. The dipole moment of chlorobenzene is lower than that of cyclohexyl chloride. S N 1 reactions are accompanied by racemization in optically active alkyl halides.
explanation: In chlorobenzene, each carbon atom is s p 2 hybridised and due to resonance there is a partial double bond character, so bond length is short. In chlorobenzene, carbon to which chlorine is attached to sp 2 hybridised and is more electronegative than the corresponding carbon in cyclohexyl chloride which is sp 3 hybridised. So the dipole moment is lower in chlorobenzene. In chlorobenzene, -I and +R effect oppose each other while in the other only -I effect is the only contributing factor resulting in lower dipole moment of cyclohexyl chloride. In S N 1 reaction, carbocation intermediate formed is a planar molecule which will lead to form d- and l- products. Hence, racemization occurs.

In chlorobenzene, carbon to which chlorine is attached to sp 2 hybridised and is more electronegative than the corresponding carbon in cyclohexyl chloride which is sp 3 hybridised. So the dipole moment is lower in chlorobenzene. In chlorobenzene, -I and +R effect oppose each other while in the other only -I effect is the only contributing factor resulting in lower dipole moment of cyclohexyl chloride. In S N 1 reaction, carbocation intermediate formed is a planar molecule which will lead to form d- and l- products. Hence, racemization occurs.
solution_html:
1. In chlorobenzene, each carbon atom is s p2 hybridised and due to resonance there is a partial double bond character, so bond length is short.
  2. In chlorobenzene, carbon to which chlorine is attached to sp2 hybridised and is more electronegative than the corresponding carbon in cyclohexyl chloride which is sp3 hybridised. So the dipole moment is lower in chlorobenzene. In chlorobenzene, -I and +R effect oppose each other while in the other only -I effect is the only contributing factor resulting in lower dipole moment of cyclohexyl chloride.
  3. In SN1 reaction, carbocation intermediate formed is a planar molecule which will lead to form d- and l- products. Hence, racemization occurs.

  1. n-butyl bromide has a higher boiling point than t-butyl bromide.
  2. Racemic mixture is optically inactive.
  3. The presence of nitro group (-NO2) at o/p-positions increases the reactivity of haloarenes towards nucleophilic substitution reactions.
question_text: Give reasons: n-butyl bromide has a higher boiling point than t-butyl bromide. Racemic mixture is optically inactive. The presence of nitro group (-NO 2 ) at o/p-positions increases the reactivity of haloarenes towards nucleophilic substitution reactions.
explanation: Higher the surface area, higher will be the boiling point of a compound. The surface area decreases with an increase in branching. That's why n-butyl bromide being (bp 375 K), a straight-chain alkyl halide has a higher boiling point than tertiary butyl bromide (bp 346 K) which is a highly branched alkyl halide. This is due to the fact that in an equimolecular mixture of enantiomeric pairs, the rotation caused by the molecules of one enantiomer is cancelled by the rotation caused by the molecules of another enantiomer. This is the reason why the racemic mixture becomes optically inactive. This is because NO 2 group at ortho and para-positions withdraw electron density from the benzene ring and thus, facilitates the nucleophilic attack on haloarenes. The carbanion thus formed is stabilized through resonance. The negative charge appeared at ortho and para position with respect to the halogen substituent is stabilised by NO 2 group.

question_text: Give reasons. C-Cl bond length in chlorobenzene is shorter than C-Cl bond length in CH 3 -Cl. The dipole moment of chlorobenzene is lower than that of cyclohexyl chloride.
explanation: In chlorobenzene, the lone pair of electrons present on Cl-atom are in conjugation with {tex}\pi{/tex} -electrons of the benzene ring. As a result, C-Cl bond acquires some double bond character while in CH 3 -Cl, bond is a pure single bond. Since, bond length of C-Cl single bond is greater than C-Cl partial double bond. Therefore, C-Cl bond in halobenzene is shorter than that in CH 3 -Cl. In chlorobenzene, the Cl-atom is linked to a sp 2 -hybridised carbon atom whereas in cyclohexyl chloride, the Cl-atom is linked to a sp 3 -hybridised carbon atom. Now, sp 2 -hybridised carbon has more s-character so, it is more electronegative, thus the density of electrons of C-Cl bond near the Cl-atom is less in chlorobenzene than in cyclohexyl chloride is more polar, i.e., it has higher dipole moment.

1. Why are alkyl halides insoluble in water?
  2. Why is butan-1-ol optically inactive but butan-2-ol is optically active?
  3. Although chlorine is an electron-withdrawing group, yet it is ortho, para- directing in electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions. Why?
question_text: Why are alkyl halides insoluble in water? Why is butan-1-ol optically inactive but butan-2-ol is optically active? Although chlorine is an electron-withdrawing group, yet it is ortho, para- directing in electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions. Why?
explanation: To be miscible with (or soluble in water) water, the solute-water force of attraction must be stronger than the solute-solute and water-water forces of attraction. Alkyl halides are polar molecules and so held together by dipole-dipole interactions. Similarly, strong H-bonds exist between the water molecules. The new force of attraction between the alkyl halides and water molecules is weaker than the forces of attraction already existing between alkyl halide-alkyl halide and water-water molecules. Hence, alkyl halides (though polar) are immiscible with (or insoluble in) water. Butan-1-ol is optically inactive compound due to the absence of asymmetric C-atom (or chiral C-atom) whereas butan-2-ol is optically active compound due to the presence of asymmetric C-atom (or chiral C-atom). Achiral compound due to the presence of similar groups on C-atoms, thus, optically inactive. Chiral compound due to the presence of different atoms on *C-atom, thus, optically active. In chlorobenzene, one of the lone pair on chlorine atom conjugates with the ring. It is the resonance hybrid of the following structures: Since, electron density is maximum at o- and p-positions due to +R-effect, therefore an electrophile can easily attack at such positions. Hence, chlorobenzene is o,p-directing in electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions.

question_html:
Which compound in each of the following pairs will react faster in SN2 reaction with OH-?

  1. CH3Br or CH3I
  2. (CH3)3CCl or CH3Cl
question_text: Which compound in each of the following pairs will react faster in S N 2 reaction with OH - ? CH 3 Br or CH 3 I (CH 3 ) 3 CCl or CH 3 Cl
explanation: In the S N 2 mechanism, the reactivity of halides for the same alkyl group increases in the order R-F << R-Cl < R-Br < R-I. This happens because as the size increases, the halide ion becomes a better leaving group. Therefore, CH 3 I will react faster than CH 3 Br in S N 2 reactions with OH - . The S N 2 mechanism involves the attack of the nucleophile at the atom bearing the leaving group. But, in case of (CH 3 ) 3 CCl,the attack of the nucleophile at the carbon atom is hindered because of the presence of bulky substituents on the carbon atom bearing the leaving group. On the other hand, there are no bulky substituents on the carbon atom bearing the leaving group in CH 3 Cl. Hence, CH 3 Cl reacts faster than (CH 3 ) 3 CCl in S N 2 reaction with OH - .

This happens because as the size increases, the halide ion becomes a better leaving group. Therefore, CH 3 I will react faster than CH 3 Br in S N 2 reactions with OH - . The S N 2 mechanism involves the attack of the nucleophile at the atom bearing the leaving group. But, in case of (CH 3 ) 3 CCl,the attack of the nucleophile at the carbon atom is hindered because of the presence of bulky substituents on the carbon atom bearing the leaving group. On the other hand, there are no bulky substituents on the carbon atom bearing the leaving group in CH 3 Cl. Hence, CH 3 Cl reacts faster than (CH 3 ) 3 CCl in S N 2 reaction with OH - .

1. In the SN2 mechanism, the reactivity of halides for the same alkyl group increases in the order R-F << R-Cl < R-Br < R-I. This happens because as the size increases, the halide ion becomes a better leaving group. Therefore, CH3I will react faster than CH3Br in SN2 reactions with OH-.
  2. The SN2 mechanism involves the attack of the nucleophile at the atom bearing the leaving group. But, in case of (CH3)3CCl,the attack of the nucleophile at the carbon atom is hindered because of the presence of bulky substituents on the carbon atom bearing the leaving group. On the other hand, there are no bulky substituents on the carbon atom bearing the leaving group in CH3Cl. Hence, CH3Cl reacts faster than (CH3)3CCl in SN2 reaction with OH-.

question_text: What are ambident nucleophiles? Explain with an example.
explanation: Ambident nucleophiles are nucleophiles having two nucleophilic sites. Thus, ambident nucleophiles have two sites through which they can attack. For example, nitrite ion is an ambident nucleophile. {tex}\left[ {{}^ - O - \ddot N = O} \right]{/tex} Nitrite ion can attack through oxygen resulting in the formation of alkyl nitrites. Also, it can attack through nitrogen resulting in the formation of nitroalkanes.

question_html:
Explain why

  1. the dipole moment of chlorobenzene is lower than that of cyclohexyl chloride?
  2. alkyl halides, though polar, are immiscible with water?
  3. Grignard reagents should be prepared under anhydrous conditions?
question_text: Explain why the dipole moment of chlorobenzene is lower than that of cyclohexyl chloride? alkyl halides, though polar, are immiscible with water? Grignard reagents should be prepared under anhydrous conditions?
explanation: In chlorobenzene, the Cl-atom is linked to a sp 2 hybridized carbon atom. In cyclohexyl chloride, the Cl-atom is linked to a sp 3 hybridized carbon atom. Now, sp 2 hybridized carbon has more s-character than sp 3 hybridized carbon atom. Therefore, the former is more electronegative than the latter. Therefore, the density of electrons of C - Cl bond near the Cl-atom is less in chlorobenzene than in cyclohexyl chloride. Moreover, the - R effect of the benzene ring of chlorobenzene decreases the electron density of the C - Cl bond near the Cl-atom. As a result, the polarity of the C - Cl bond in chlorobenzene decreases. Hence, the dipole moment of chlorobenzene is lower than that of cyclohexyl chloride. To be miscible with water, the solute-water force of attraction must be stronger than the solute-solute and water-water forces of attraction. Alkyl halides are polar molecules and so held together by dipole-dipole interactions. Similarly, strong H-bonds exist between the water molecules. The new force of attraction between the alkyl halides and water molecules is weaker than the alkyl halide-alkyl halide and water-water forces of attraction. Hence, alkyl halides (though polar) are immiscible with water. Grignard reagents are very reactive. In the presence of moisture, they react to give alkanes. {tex}\mathop {\mathop R\limits^{\delta - } \mathop M\limits^{\delta + } g\mathop X\limits^{\delta - } }\limits_{Grigrard\;reagent} + {H_2}O \to \mathop {R - H}\limits_{Alkane} + Mg(OH)X{/tex} Therefore, Grignard reagents should be prepared under anhydrous conditions.

In the presence of moisture, they react to give alkanes. {tex}\mathop {\mathop R\limits^{\delta - } \mathop M\limits^{\delta + } g\mathop X\limits^{\delta - } }\limits_{Grigrard\;reagent} + {H_2}O \to \mathop {R - H}\limits_{Alkane} + Mg(OH)X{/tex} Therefore, Grignard reagents should be prepared under anhydrous conditions.
solution_html:
1. In chlorobenzene, the Cl-atom is linked to a sp2 hybridized carbon atom. In cyclohexyl chloride, the Cl-atom is linked to a sp3 hybridized carbon atom. As a result, the polarity of the C - Cl bond in chlorobenzene decreases. Hence, the dipole moment of chlorobenzene is lower than that of cyclohexyl chloride.
  2. To be miscible with water, the solute-water force of attraction must be stronger than the solute-solute and water-water forces of attraction. Alkyl halides are polar molecules and so held together by dipole-dipole interactions. Similarly, strong H-bonds exist between the water molecules. The new force of attraction between the alkyl halides and water molecules is weaker than the alkyl halide-alkyl halide and water-water forces of attraction. question_text: Account for the following : The C-Cl bond length in chlorobenzene is shorter than that in CH 3 -Cl. Chloroform is stored in closed dark brown bottles.
explanation: Due to delocalization of lone pairs of electrons of the Cl atom over the benzene ring, C-Cl bond in chlorobenzene acquires double bond character due to resonance effect. While in methyl chloride, CH 3 -Cl, C-Cl bond is a pure single bond. This is because in the case of CH 3 Cl no resonance is involved. Therefore, C-Cl bond in chlorobenzene is shorter than in CH 3 -Cl. In the presence of air and sunlight, chloroform gets oxidised to phosgene (carbonyl chloride, COCl 2 ), a poisonous compound. Thus, to prevent the formation of phosgene, chloroform is stored in dark brown bottles filled up to brim. {tex}CHC{l_3} + \frac{1}{2}{O_2}\xrightarrow{{hv}}\mathop {COC{l_2} + }\limits_{\mathop {Carbonylchloride}\limits_{(Phosgene)} } HCl{/tex}

question_html: Chlorobenzene is extremely less reactive towards a nucleophilic substitution reaction. Give two reasons for the same.
question_text: Chlorobenzene is extremely less reactive towards a nucleophilic substitution reaction. Give two reasons for the same.
explanation: Two reasons for the less reactivity of chlorobenzene towards a nucleophilic substitution reaction are as follows: Resonance Effect: The electron pair of the chlorine atom is involved in conjugation with the {tex}\pi{/tex} -electrons of the benzene ring and the following resonating structures are obtained: The contributing structures III, IV and V indicate that C-Cl bond has partial double character. As a result, the C-Cl bond in chlorobenzene is shorter and hence, stronger. Thus, cleavage of C-Cl bond in benzene becomes difficult which makes it less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution. Increased Electron Density: A repulsion is suffered by the nucleophile due to increased electron density on the benzene ring which prohibits the nucleophile to make close access for the attack on the molecule.

As a result, the C-Cl bond in chlorobenzene is shorter and hence, stronger. Thus, cleavage of C-Cl bond in benzene becomes difficult which makes it less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution. Increased Electron Density: A repulsion is suffered by the nucleophile due to increased electron density on the benzene ring which prohibits the nucleophile to make close access for the attack on the molecule.
solution_html:
Two reasons for the less reactivity of chlorobenzene towards a nucleophilic substitution reaction are as follows:

  1. Resonance Effect: The electron pair of the chlorine atom is involved in conjugation with the {tex}\pi{/tex}-electrons of the benzene ring and the following resonating structures are obtained:
    The contributing structures III, IV and V indicate that C-Cl bond has partial double character. As a result, the C-Cl bond in chlorobenzene is shorter and hence, stronger. Thus, cleavage of C-Cl bond in benzene becomes difficult which makes it less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution.
  2. Increased Electron Density: A repulsion is suffered by the nucleophile due to increased electron density on the benzene ring which prohibits the nucleophile to make close access for the attack on the molecule.

question_html:
Explain why

  1. alkyl halides, though polar, are immiscible with water?
  2. Grignard's reagent should be prepared under anhydrous conditions?
question_text: Explain why alkyl halides, though polar, are immiscible with water? Grignard's reagent should be prepared under anhydrous conditions?
explanation: Both alkyl halides and water are polar molecules. The intermolecular force of attractions between two alkyl halide molecules is dipole-dipole attraction. While The intermolecular force of attractions between two water molecules is by hydrogen bonding. When alkyl halides are mixed with water a new force of attraction between the alkyl halides and water comes into play, this new force of attraction is much weaker than the forces of attractions already existing between two alkyl halides and two water molecules respectively. This is the reason that alkyl halides, though polar, are immiscible with water. In Grignard reagent, the carbon-magnesium bond is highly polar, with carbon pulling electrons from electropositive magnesium. The magnesium halogen bond is essentially ionic in nature. Thus, a Grignard reagent is a very versatile or very reactive reagent which can react with any source of a proton to give hydrocarbons. If even traces water is present in the reaction, then they will react with the reagent producing the corresponding hydrocarbon thus making the reagent ineffective. Therefore, Grignard's reagents should be prepared under anhydrous conditions.

question_text: Although chlorine is an electron withdrawing group, yet it is ortho-, para- directing in electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions. Why?
explanation: Chlorine is electron withdrawing in nature but due to the strong +R effect, one of the lone pair on chlorine atom conjugates with the benzene ring in chlorobenzene. So, it is the resonance hybrid of the following structures: It is clear from resonating structures of chlorobenzene, the electron density is maximum at o- and p-positions due to +R- effect. Therefore an electrophile (E + ) can easily attack at such positions. Cl donates its lone pair of electrons to the aromatic ring. Hence, chlorobenzene is o,p-directing towards electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions.

question_text: Write a chemical test to distinguish between chlorobenzene and benzylchloride chloroform and carbon tetrachloride Why is methyl chloride hydrolysed more easily than chlorobenzene?
explanation: Benzyl chloride when treated with AgNO 3 solution, will give a white ppt. while chlorobenzene will not. Chloroform when treated with 1 o amine and KOH, gives the offensive smell of isocyanides while CCl 4 does not give this test. Chlorobenzene is stabilised by resonance so, it has a double bond character between C and Cl. Secondly, C is sp 2 -hybridised so more electronegative, thus Cl - is not replaced easily. While in methyl chloride, C- Cl has a single bond character which can be cleaved easily and hence, it is hydrolysed more easily than chlorobenzene.

explanation: DDT is used as an insecticide and iodoform (CHI 3 ) is used as a mild antiseptic. Reactivity of S N 2 reactions depends on steric hindrance. S N 2 reactivity is inversely proportional to the steric hindrance. 1-bromopentane will react faster because it is a primary alkyl halide and hence less sterically hindered. Out of 1-bromo-2-methylbutane and 2-bromo-2-methylbutane, 1-bromo-2-methylbutane is more reactive as it is a primary carbon where the attack of nucleophile is more feasible whereas in the second case, due to the stability of tertiary carbocation, it will prefer to undergo S N 1 substitution reaction.

question_html: How would you differentiate between SN1 and SN2 mechanism of substitution reactions? Give one example of each.
question_text: How would you differentiate between S N 1 and S N 2 mechanism of substitution reactions? Give one example of each.
explanation: In S N 1 mechanism of substitution reaction, the rate of reaction depends upon the concentration of only one reactant, e.g. in the reaction between tert-butyl bromide and hydroxide ion to form tert-butyl alcohol, rate of reaction depends only on the concentration of tert-butyl bromide. (CH 3 ) 3 C-Br+OH - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (CH 3 ) 3 C-OH+Br - It involves the formation of the carbocation intermediate and racemisation takes place. In S N 2 mechanism of substitution reaction, the rate of reaction depends upon the concentration of both the reactants i.e. on both haloalkane and nucleophile, e.g. in the reaction between methyl chloride and hydroxide ion to form methanol, rate of reaction depends on the concentration of both the reactants. It involves the formation of transition state and inversion (Walden inversion) of configuration take place.
solution: In S N 1 mechanism of substitution reaction, the rate of reaction depends upon the concentration of only one reactant, e.g. in the reaction between tert-butyl bromide and hydroxide ion to form tert-butyl alcohol, rate of reaction depends only on the concentration of tert-butyl bromide. (CH 3 ) 3 C-Br+OH - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (CH 3 ) 3 C-OH+Br - It involves the formation of the carbocation intermediate and racemisation takes place. In S N 2 mechanism of substitution reaction, the rate of reaction depends upon the concentration of both the reactants i.e. on both haloalkane and nucleophile, e.g. in the reaction between methyl chloride and hydroxide ion to form methanol, rate of reaction depends on the concentration of both the reactants. It involves the formation of transition state and inversion (Walden inversion) of configuration take place.

In SN1 mechanism of substitution reaction, the rate of reaction depends upon the concentration of only one reactant, e.g. in the reaction between tert-butyl bromide and hydroxide ion to form tert-butyl alcohol, rate of reaction depends only on the concentration of tert-butyl bromide. (CH3)3C-Br+OH-{tex}\rightarrow{/tex}(CH3)3C-OH+Br- It involves the formation of the carbocation intermediate and racemisation takes place.
In SN2 mechanism of substitution reaction, the rate of reaction depends upon the concentration of both the reactants i.e. on both haloalkane and nucleophile, e.g. in the reaction between methyl chloride and hydroxide ion to form methanol, rate of reaction depends on the concentration of both the reactants.

It involves the formation of transition state and inversion (Walden inversion) of configuration take place.

Now, sp 2 -hybridised carbon has mores-character so, it is more electronegative, thus the density of electrons of C-Cl bond near the Cl-atom is less in chlorobenzene than in cyclohexyl chloride. Hence, the C-Cl bond of cyclohexyl chloride is more polar, i.e. it has a higher dipole moment. To be miscible with (or soluble in water) water, the solute-water force of attraction must be stronger than the solute-solute and water-water forces of attraction. Alkyl halides are polar molecules and so held together by dipole-dipole interactions. Similarly, strong H-bonds exist between the water molecules. The new force of attraction between the alkyl halides and water molecules is weaker than the forces of attraction already existing between alkyl halide-alkyl halide and water-water molecules. Hence, alkyl halides (though polar) are immiscible with (or insoluble in) water. CH 3 I will react faster as compared to CH 3 Br because CH 3 I has lower bond dissociation energy and I - is a better leaving group than Br - . Thus, in the presence of nucleophile, iodide ion will be released at a faster rate. In S N 2 reactions, reactivity depends upon the steric hindrance CH 3 Cl being a 1° halide will react faster as compared to (CH 3 ) 3 CCl which is a 3° halide because 1° halides undergo S N 2 mechanism faster than 3° halides.

solution_html:
1. In chlorobenzene, the Cl-atom is linked to an sp2-hybridised carbon atom whereas, in cyclohexyl chloride, the Cl-atom is linked to an sp3-hybridised carbon atom. Now, sp2-hybridised carbon has mores-character so, it is more electronegative, thus the density of electrons of C-Cl bond near the Cl-atom is less in chlorobenzene than in cyclohexyl chloride. Hence, the C-Cl bond of cyclohexyl chloride is more polar, i.e. it has a higher dipole moment.
  2. To be miscible with (or soluble in water) water, the solute-water force of attraction must be stronger than the solute-solute and water-water forces of attraction. Alkyl halides are polar molecules and so held together by dipole-dipole interactions. Similarly, strong H-bonds exist between the water molecules. The new force of attraction between the alkyl halides and water molecules is weaker than the forces of attraction already existing between alkyl halide-alkyl halide and water-water molecules. Hence, alkyl halides (though polar) are immiscible with (or insoluble in) water.
  3. CH3I will react faster as compared to CH3Br because CH3I has lower bond dissociation energy and I- is a better leaving group than Br-. Thus, in the presence of nucleophile, iodide ion will be released at a faster rate.

question_text: Suggest a possible reason for the following observations: The order of reactivity of haloalkanes is RI > RBr > RCI. neo-pentyl chloride, (CH 3 ) 3 C-CH 2 Cl does not follow S N 2 mechanism.
explanation: When alkyl group is the same, the reactivity of alkyl halide is decided by the halogen present. Greater the size of a halogen atom, larger is the R-X bond length, and weaker is the bond. Thus, more reactive is alkyl halide. On going down the group size of the halides increases. The order of the size of halogen is F < Cl <Br < I Thus, the order of reactivity is R-I > R-Br > R-Cl In neopentyl chloride, the leaving group is Chloride (Cl - ) ion therefore, the nucleophile will attack the C atom attached to Cl atom from the back side, according S N 2 mechanism. As we know for S N 2 mechanism to proceed the C atom at which the nucleophile will attach itself should be unhindered but in neopentyl this C atom is sterically hindered due to presence of three bulky -CH 3 groups which do not allow the nucleophile to approach the C atom to which leaving group is attached. Hence, the S N 2 mechanism is not followed by neopentyl chloride. {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_2}Cl} - C{H_3}{/tex} Neopentyl chloride

1. When alkyl group is the same, the reactivity of alkyl halide is decided by the halogen present. Greater the size of a halogen atom, larger is the R-X bond length, and weaker is the bond. Thus, more reactive is alkyl halide. On going down the group size of the halides increases.
    The order of the size of halogen is
    F < Cl <Br < I
    Thus, the order of reactivity is R-I > R-Br > R-Cl
  2. In neopentyl chloride, the leaving group is Chloride (Cl-) ion therefore, the nucleophile will attack the C atom attached to Cl atom from the back side, according SN2 mechanism. As we know for SN2 mechanism to proceed the C atom at which the nucleophile will attach itself should be unhindered but in neopentyl this C atom is sterically hindered due to presence of three bulky -CH3 groups which do not allow the nucleophile to approach the C atom to which leaving group is attached. Hence, the SN2 mechanism is not followed by neopentyl chloride.

question_text: Discuss the mechanism of S N 1 reaction of haloalkanes.
explanation: Tertiary halides undergo nucleophilic substitution through S N 1 mechanism. It is a two step mechanism. First step involves formation of carbocation by loss of halide ion. 1 st Step: {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{Cl}^{C{H_3}} - C{H_3}\xrightarrow{{slow}}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^| }\limits_ \oplus ^{C{H_3}} - C{H_3}+C{l^ - }{/tex} 2 nd Step: Second step involves attack of nucleophile which can happen from either side of the plane of the molecule Thus, the compound which gives the most stable carbocation on losing the halide ion will preferably undergo nucleophilic substitution by S N 1 mechanism.

solution_html:
Tertiary halides undergo nucleophilic substitution through SN1 mechanism. It is a two step mechanism. First step involves formation of carbocation by loss of halide ion.
1st Step:
{tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{Cl}^{C{H_3}} - C{H_3}\xrightarrow{{slow}}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^| }\limits_ \oplus ^{C{H_3}} - C{H_3}+C{l^ - }{/tex}
2nd Step: Second step involves attack of nucleophile which can happen from either side of the plane of the molecule

Thus, the compound which gives the most stable carbocation on losing the halide ion will preferably undergo nucleophilic substitution by SN1 mechanism.
